data.title: Insider trades: Nvidia, Tesla among notable names this week
data.description:
This week, major insider trades were witnessed across companies including Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) and Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO). The following transactions occurred between September 15 and September 19.

  • Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) Director Stephen Neal sold 28,000 shares at $177.58 each, in a transaction worth nearly $5 million. Following the transaction, he now holds 184,867 shares in the firm.
  • Walmart’s (NYSE:WMT) Chief Technology Officer Suresh Kumar disposed of 30,000 shares for $103.47 apiece, totaling $3.1 million. His stake stands at 1.79 million shares.
  • Mark Brazeal, chief legal officer at Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO), sold a combined 27,204 shares across various transactions at prices ranging from $ 343.13 to $365.15, for a total value of $9.59 million. His ownership has now reduced to 329,302 shares in the company.
  • Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) Director William Parrett sold 11,500 shares at $306, in a transaction valued at $3.52 million, leaving him with 27,075 shares.
  • Lilly Endowment sold 106,626 shares of Eli Lilly (NYSE:LLY) in the price range of $763.29 to $767.06 for a total value of $81.53 million. It retains ownership of 95.63 million shares in the company.
  • On the buy side, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) CEO Elon Musk purchased shares worth nearly $1 billion. He bought 2.57M shares at an average price of $389.28, raising his total holdings to about 509 million shares.
  • Strategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) EVP & General Counsel Wei-Ming Shao acquired 12,500 shares at $96.92 each, in a deal worth $1.21M. The purchase has brought his total holdings in the firm to 31,753 shares.

data.title: RTX unit Collins Aerospace resolving cybersecurity event after EU air traffic disruption
data.description:
Collins Aerospace, a check-in and boarding system provider owned by RTX Corporation (NYSE:RTX), announced Saturday that it is resolving a cybersecurity incident that has disrupted passenger services at several major European airports, including London's Heathrow.

“Collins Aerospace, who provide check-in and boarding systems for several airlines across multiple airports globally, is experiencing a technical issue, which may cause delays for departing passengers,” said a spokesperson for Heathrow, the continent’s busiest airport.

Other European airports, including Brussels Airport in Belgium and Berlin Airport in Germany, also reported an impact. Several hours later, Dublin Airport and Cork Airport, Ireland’s largest and second-largest airports, respectively, also reported minor issues from the incident.

"We have become aware of a cyber-related disruption to our MUSE software in select airports. We are actively working to resolve the issue and restore full functionality to our customers as quickly as possible,” Collins Aerospace said in a statement.

“The impact is limited to electronic customer check-in and baggage drop and can be mitigated with manual check-in operations. We will share more details as they are available."

data.title: Trending stocks as Wall Street cheers the Fed’s long-awaited rate cut
data.description:
Wall Street closed higher Friday, marking its third straight weekly gain, after the Federal Reserve delivered a long-awaited 25 basis-point rate cut—the first in nine months. The Fed’s updated projections also signaled a more dovish stance on interest rates for the rest of the year.

For the week, the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) added +1.2%, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) gained +1.1%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq (COMP:IND) advanced +2.2%.

Here’s a look at stocks that caught investor attention this week:

Nvidia (NVDA) and Intel (INTC) announced a blockbuster deal on Thursday to co-develop PC and data center chips. Simultaneously, Nvidia said it would take a $5B stake in Intel. Separately, Nvidia (NVDA) was in the spotlight after reports emerged on Wednesday that the Cyberspace Administration of China has banned the nation's largest technology companies from buying Nvidia’s (NVDA) AI chips.

Workday (WDAY) gained after activist investor Elliott Investment Management said it has built a stake of more than $2 billion in the human resources software provider. Elliott said it was pleased with its dialogue with the team and voiced confidence that the multi-year strategy shared at the company’s Financial Analyst Day will deliver strong long-term value for shareholders.

Palantir Technologies (PLTR) entered a partnership with the U.K. that would see an investment of up to £1.5B in the U.K. to deliver new jobs, growth and national security. The partnership will identify opportunities with U.K. Defense, which could be worth up to £750M over the next five years. Palantir plans to make the U.K. its European headquarters for defense and create up to 350 new high-skilled jobs.

CrowdStrike (CRWD) rose nearly 14% over the week after outlining a 20%+ Y/Y net new ARR growth target (~22% YoY ARR growth) for FY27, which is above consensus estimates of 12% Y/Y (~20% Y/Y ARR growth). The cybersecurity firm also indicated it expects net new annual recurring revenue to increase by at least 40% during the second half of fiscal 2026.

Novo Nordisk (NVO) gained nearly 9% after a real-world study showed its diabetes drug Ozempic outperformed Eli Lilly’s (LLY) older drug Trulicity (dulaglutide) in certain diabetic and heart disease patients. The analysis of nearly 60,000 Medicare patients aged 66 and older with type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and other health conditions showed Ozempic (once-weekly semaglutide) cut the risk of major cardiovascular events such as heart attack or stroke by 23% and lowered the risk of death by 26% compared to Trulicity.

Eli Lilly (LLY), meanwhile, said that data from its phase 3, head-to-head study showed that its oral GLP-1 drug, orforglipron, was more effective against Novo Nordisk's (NVO) oral semaglutide in adults with type 2 diabetes. The drugmaker also announced detailed results from a Phase 3 trial in which its GIP/GLP-1 dual receptor agonist, tirzepatide, marketed as Mounjaro, reached the primary endpoint in children and adolescents with type 2 diabetes.

Bullish (BLSH) advanced 33% over the week as it issued stronger-than-expected guidance for Q3 revenue and delivered Q2 earnings and sales that came in better than expected. The crypto exchange, which went public last month, expects Q3 adjusted revenue of $69.0M-$76.0M, above $57.1M average analyst estimate.

FactSet Research Systems (FDS) fell over 20% after reporting weaker-than-expected fiscal Q4 earnings and issuing soft FY26 guidance. Q4 adjusted EPS of $4.05 trailed the $4.13 consensus. The company expects FY26 GAAP revenue of $2.42B–$2.45B (midpoint $2.44B), below the $2.45B consensus. Organic annual subscription value is projected to grow 4%–6%.

DexCom (DXCM) dropped nearly 12% in the wake of a short report from Hunterbrook Capital. Despite the report, the continuous glucose monitoring equipment maker managed to retain its bullish views on Wall Street, with TD Cowen and Baird among the firms to reiterate their buy-equivalent ratings on the stock.

Rocket Lab USA (RKLB) fell nearly 11% after the company entered into a $750 ATM equity offering with underwriters. The program allows the company to sell up to $750 million of its stock over time through several different investment banks.

data.title: PJT Partners being picked to lead TalkTalk break-up: Sky News
data.description:
Privately owned British telecommunications provider TalkTalk Group is hiring U.S. investment banker PJT Partners (NYSE:PJT) to conduct a strategic review that will lead to the sale of one of the biggest broadband providers in the country, Sky News reported on Saturday.

As part of the review, New York-based PJT Partners (NYSE:PJT) is expected to determine the most favorable timing to divest TalkTalk's remaining businesses, according to the report, which also said that the company is set to finalize its appointment shortly.

Any transaction will come under heavy scrutiny of the British telecom regulator, Ofcom, as TalkTalk, with 3.2M residential broadband customers in the U.K., remains one of the largest broadband providers in the country.

The review will lead to separate sales of TalkTalk's consumer operations and its wholesale and network division, PlatformX. According to insiders, plans are underway to seek a standalone sale of the company's Ethernet subsidiary, which comes under PlatformX.

TalkTalk was taken private in 2020 in a £1.1B deal led by its second-largest shareholder, Toscafund.

data.title: Crypto firms, Argentine banks record biggest swings: weekly financials wrap
data.description:
Crypto-linked firms and Argentine banks were among some of the biggest financial movers in the past week, which featured a Federal Reserve meeting that saw the first interest-rate cut this year.

Figure Technology Solutions, the blockchain-based lender that went public earlier in September, surged 38%, even as crypto prices recorded modest weekly losses;

Bitcoin (BTC-USD) miner CleanSpark (CLSK) gapped up 32%;

Brighthouse Financial (BHF) jumped 18% after a report that a consortium led by insurer Aquarian Holdings is in advanced discussions to pay $65 to $70 a share for Brighthouse;

Insurance company Lemonade (LMND) climbed 16%; and 

Stablecoin issuer Circle Internet Group (CRCL), which during the week partnered with crypto platform Kraken, rounded out the five biggest financial winners (with market cap over $2B), gaining 15%.

On the losers, crypto exchange Gemini Space Station (GEMI), which debuted last week, took the lead, tumbling 25%;

Factset Research Systems (FDS) slid 20% after turning in fiscal Q4 earnings that missed the average analyst estimate, in addition to soft revenue guidance for fiscal 2026;

Argentine banks Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL) and Banco Macro (BMA) fell 12% and 10%, respectively; and

FS KKR Capital (FSK), which during the week priced a $400M notes offering, retreated 9.3%.

data.title: Notable analyst calls this week: Tesla, Union Pacific, and Intel among top picks
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) closed in the green on Friday, fueled by fresh rate cuts from the Federal Reserve.

For the week, both Nasdaq (COMP:IND) and Dow (DJI) gained 0.5%.

Wall Street had a slew of upgrades and downgrades from analysts. Here are some of the major calls for the week:

Union Pacific upgraded to Buy by Citi

Union Pacific (NYSE:UNP) was upgraded by Citi to Buy from Neutral, with the brokerage recommending that investors look at Union Pacific as a stock set for gains.

The firm set a PT of $251 on UNP as analyst Ariel Rosa said the stock was too compelling to ignore. Rosa pointed to more clarity on Union Pacific's planned merger with Norfolk Southern, even though a contentious merger review process is anticipated.

"The good news for UNP investors is that the attractiveness of UNP shares does not hinge on deal approval, as UNP trades at a discount to its long-term average PE on a stand-alone basis, even as it executes at a high level," said Rosa.

Tesla lands bull rating from Baird

Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) was upgraded by Baird to an Outperform from Neutral, saying that the electric vehicle maker is increasingly viewed by the investing public as the leader in physical AI.

"Relatively muted stock reactions following a series of less-than-stellar quarters and investor inbounds regarding long-term initiatives lead us to believe focus has increasingly shifted to the future for TSLA," highlighted analyst Ben Kallo.

The brokerage expects some near-term choppiness with fundamentals but thinks sentiment has skewed positive.

Intel downgraded by Citi after Nvidia deal

Citi downgraded Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) to Sell from Neutral, following the chipmaker’s deal with Nvidia to co-develop PC and data center chips.

“We expect minimal improvement for Intel as better graphics won't make Intel's CPU better than AMD's given the processor is the main performance driver,” said analyst Christopher Danely, adding that the brokerage don't expect much from the AI product “given the small [total addressable market] ($1 to $2 billion) and our belief that the stock is pricing in success in its leading-edging foundry business, which we believe has minimal chance to succeed.”

However, the brokerage upped PT to $29 from $24 and said investors are likely pricing in a deal for Intel's foundry.

Hershey receives double upgrade from Goldman

Goldman Sachs double upgraded Hershey (NYSE:HSY) to Buy from Sell with a 30% hike in its price target to $222.

With cost pressures already priced in, and market trends improving in the company’s favor, Hershey shares are now priced with a “compelling” risk/reward.

“Hershey emerges as one of the more insulated packaged food portfolios from private label competition given the low penetration of the confection category overall,” said Goldman Sachs analyst Leah Jordan, adding that pricing actions should also serve as a tailwind to the second half of 2025 and into 2026 given better-than-expected elasticities year-to-date.

UBS sees limited near-term upside for Box

UBS downgraded Box (NYSE:BOX) to Neutral from Buy as the brokerage finds its core business to be stable, but its near-term upside is limited.

"Based on our conversations with ~10 checks we believe spending trends appear neutral heading into 2026, with greater optimism emerging for 2027," said UBS analyst Seth Gilbert, who also reduced PT on the stock to $36 from $42.

The firm believes Box can generate more upside entering 2027 as customers upgrade to its artificial intelligence-powered Enterprise Advanced offering.

CoreWeave sees bullish views at Raymond James, Citizens

Citizens upgraded CoreWeave (NASDAQ:CRWV) to Market Outperform from Market Perform and set a price target of $180 on the stock. Raymond James also began coverage of the company with an Outperform rating with a $130 PT.

Raymond analysts led by Josh Beck said the rating and price target reflect "our bullish stance on the duration of scaling laws due to reasoning vector growth and broadening inference commercialization with AI 6 capex Street estimates that appear too low versus our bottom-up build expectations."

Citizens analysts noted that graphics processing unit as a service, or GPUaaS', total addressable market, or TAM, is accelerating.

TD Cowen downgraded Warner. Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) to Hold from Buy, citing deal uncertainty related to a potential bid from Paramount Skydance (NASDAQ:PSKY). "While PSKY may come in with a $20+ bid, we don't love the risk-reward here given the potential for WBD shares to quickly round-trip to $11-$12 if the bid doesn't materialize," TD Cowen analyst Doug Creutz said.

Mizuho Securities raised PT on Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) to $182 from $155 ahead of the company's quarterly results next week. Analyst Vijay Rakesh said that ahead of Micron's fiscal fourth quarter results, they have raised Micron's High Bandwidth Memory, or HBM, estimates and price target combined with a constructive Dynamic Random-Access Memory, or DRAM, and NAND market.

Morgan Stanley upgraded Klaviyo (NYSE:KVYO) to Overweight from Equal-weight and raised PT to $50 from $40. The brokerage sees Klaviyo positioned to sustain durable 20%+ growth as its market opportunity expands from email marketing to a broader CRM platform for B2C businesses.

data.title: Comcast reportedly weighing layoffs at its biggest unit
data.description:
Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) is considering staff reductions at its biggest unit, Connectivity & Platforms, which runs its Xfinity internet, mobile, and pay TV operations. Reuters reported, citing a source familiar with the matter.

The layoffs will be part of a restructuring initiative that the company expects to begin in January to eliminate a management layer between its corporate and regional offices in an attempt to streamline the unit’s management.

Comcast’s (NASDAQ:CMCSA) Connectivity & Platforms division, which also includes the company's Sky brand, currently operates under a three-tier management structure where division heads serve as a link between the regional heads and headquarters.

As part of the revamped setup, the regional teams will report directly to a new executive overseeing nationwide operations, a memo sent to employees indicates. The source said that while there would be layoffs, Comcast (NASDAQ:CMCSA) was still finalizing which positions needed to be centralized.

In the memo, the company didn’t disclose how many roles would be eliminated but stated that customer-facing frontline teams, such as those focused on customer service and retail, would not be impacted.

"This change is not a reflection of anyone's contributions—it is about simplifying how we work so we can compete more effectively," the memo read.

data.title: Tesla granted regulatory nod to test autonomous robotaxis in Arizona
data.description:
• Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) has received approval from the Arizona Department of Transportation to begin testing its autonomous robotaxi vehicles in the state with a safety monitor, multiple news outlets reported late Friday.
  • The EV maker reached out to the agency in June to start the certification process for an autonomous vehicle ride-sharing service, and the company now expects to roll out the test in the Phoenix Metro area using safety drivers, Reuters reported, quoting an email from ADOT.
  • In June, Tesla (NASDAQ:TSLA) launched a test of a limited version of its highly anticipated robotaxi service targeting a small group of company enthusiasts, investors, and social media influencers in Austin, Texas.
  • Earlier this month, its robotaxi ambitions got a further boost when Nevada’s Department of Motor Vehicles approved Tesla’s (NASDAQ:TSLA) plans to begin testing its autonomous vehicles in the state.

data.title: Novartis CEO reportedly weighing U.S. price cuts as Trump’s MFN deadline nears
data.description:
Just days ahead of the Trump administration’s Sept. 29 deadline, which requires Big Pharma to lower U.S. drug prices to the level of other developed nations, Novartis (NYSE:NVS) CEO Vas Narasimhan has told a German daily that the company is actively considering the pricing proposals.

“We’re working with the government to find constructive solutions so Americans pay less for their medicines,” Narasimhan said in an interview with Neue Zürcher Zeitung on Saturday. The Swiss drugmaker is looking at eliminating the price gap that exists between its U.S. products and those sold in other industrialized nations.

His remarks came after U.S. President Donald Trump sent letters to the CEOs of 17 leading drugmakers, including Narasimhan, in July, urging them to adjust the U.S. prices of their pharmaceutical products to match those in other developed countries, in line with his most-favored-nation pricing policy.

Trump gave the executives 60 days to meet his demands and, if they fail to comply, threatened to “deploy every tool in our arsenal to protect American families from continued abusive drug pricing practices.” 

Narasimhan said that his company is not revising its guidance despite Trump’s demands. Novartis (OTCPK:NVSEF) set its mid-term sales outlook at 5% CAGR for 2024–2029 in November 2024. 

“We continue to believe that profits will grow faster than revenue. Of course, if something were to change dramatically, we would have to make adjustments. But no matter what happens, under current US law, any changes should be manageable,” he added.

In its home country, Switzerland, drug prices are extremely low and at the lower end of the scale in comparison to other OECD countries, Narasimhan noted.

Amid a potential 15% tariff being considered by the Trump administration for EU-made pharmaceuticals, the CEO said, “We have significantly increased our stockpiles in the US, so they will certainly last until mid-2026.”

He added that Novartis (NYSE:NVS) expects the levies to primarily target the active pharmaceutical ingredients of drugs, and its API exports from Switzerland to the U.S. are limited.

“So, if there were indeed a specific Swiss pharmaceutical tariff, we could easily handle it,” Narasimhan noted. “We are more concerned about the tariffs for the entire pharmaceutical industry than the specific Swiss situation,”

data.title: Oil prices slide as market shrugs off expanded E.U. sanctions on Russia
data.description:
Crude futures fell Friday for the third straight day, erasing gains made early in the week following Ukrainian attacks on Russia's energy infrastructure, hitting key refineries and export facilities, including the Primorsk terminal.

Russian oil exports to China and India declined for the second week in a row, analysts said, raising the prospect that - if the trend continues - Russia ultimately could be forced to cut oil production.

The European Union unveiled a new package of sanctions Friday against Chinese and other foreign companies buying Russian oil, intending to show President Trump the bloc is ramping up economic pressure on Russia and its backers.

The package - the E.U.'s 19th since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine - also includes sanctions against Russia's energy, banking and crypto sectors, and speed up its phaseout of purchases of Russian liquefied natural gas a year earlier than previously planned, President Ursula von der Leyen said.

The new measures also include a lower crude oil price cap of $47.60/bbl, and new sanctions against vessels carrying oil subject to Western restrictions.

The package will need the backing of all 27 member states - which is far from guaranteed - and Mizuho's Robert Yawger noted the previous 18 rounds of E.U. sanctions "have proven largely ineffective in stopping the flow of Russian oil exports."

"The balancing act of OPEC+ oversupplying the global market against a possible drop in Russian oil sales is keeping crude futures in very tight trade," ROK Financial's Dennis Kissler said in a note.

Earlier this week, the U.S. Federal Reserve reduced interest rates by 0.25% and signaled two more reductions this year, but "the Fed's action is not translating to growth for the crude market due to underlying market fundamentals," Again Capital's John Kilduff wrote, adding that further quarter-point rate cuts likely would not help oil markets since they would further weaken the dollar, making oil more expensive to buy.

In Citi's quarterly outlook, the bank's analysts forecast Brent crude easing toward $60/bbl by the end of this year and into 2026, as the OPEC+ decision to start unwinding a second layer of production cuts adds to expected global stock builds, which the bank now pegs at 1.1M bbl/day in 2025 and 2.1M bbl/day in 2026.

Citi said a bear case that could push Brent toward $50/bbl might happen if global demand erodes on weaker macroeconomics and trade, supply rises faster than expected, and if China stockpiling disappoints.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for October delivery finished Friday -1.4% to $62.68/bbl, front-month November Brent crude (CO1:COM) settled -1.1% to $66.68/bbl, and front-month October Nymex natural gas (NG1:COM) closed -1.7% to $2.888/MMBtu.

For the week, Nymex crude finished flat, Brent fell 0.4%, and U.S. natural gas dropped 1.8%.

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:USO), (BNO), (NYSEARCA:UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (NYSEARCA:XLE)

Energy stocks, as represented by the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund (NYSEARCA:XLE), ended roughly unchanged.

Top 20 gainers in energy and natural resources in the past 5 days: New Fortress Energy (NFE) +78.6%, Oklo (OKLO) +63.5%, Plug Power (PLUG) +43.4%, Flux Power (FLUX) +42.5%, American Battery Technology (ABAT) +42.2%, American Resources (AREC) +41.4%, Nano Nuclear Energy (NNE) +40.1%, Energy Vault (NRGV) +35.1%, Fluence Energy (FLNC) +33.7%, Anfield Energy (AEC) +33.3%, Centrus Energy (LEU) +32.6%, Ballard Power (BLDP) +32.4%, U.S. Antimony (AUMY) +31.6%, Lightbridge (LTBR) +30.7%, Nuscale Power (SMR) +28.7%, Bloom Energy (BE) +26.3%, Ramaco Resources (METC) +25.4%, Encore Energy (EU) +25.1%, Energy Fuels (UUUU) +24.5%, Enovix (ENVX) +23.9%, Peabody Energy (BTU) +23.4%.

Source: Barchart.com
